title: How visualisations work
subtitle: Specific imagery the body can do something with, not abstract scenes of abundance.
excerpt: A reference article for the visualisation practices in the library. Covers what a visualisation is in this context, what makes a scene specific enough to land, the sensory anchors used in the practice scripts, and how to close a session. Read once; the practice scripts assume it.
sourceNotes: Guided visualisation has multiple lineages, sports-psychology mental rehearsal, hypnotherapy traditions, modern adaptations of yoga nidra, and the imagery work woven through cognitive-behavioural therapy. The specific-imagery rule used here follows that received craft. The use of visualisation alongside tapping and energy statements is drawn from Rebecca J Page's MONEY (2025), SLEEP (2025), and MANIFESTING (2025) programs.

content.text: A visualisation in this library is a guided imagining: a specific scene held in the mind for three to ten minutes, with enough sensory detail that the body responds as if the scene were happening. Not a vague "picture abundance", a concrete image with edges. The reservoir refilling itself. The wallet sitting heavy on the bedside table. The bank balance number, seen and held without flinching.

This reading covers what a visualisation is here, what makes a scene specific enough to land, the sensory anchors used in the practice scripts, and how to close a session. The individual visualisation scripts assume it.
What a visualisation is here

A held scene, kept in mind for the length of the practice. The scene has detail: a specific room, a specific object, a specific point of view. The practice is the steady holding, letting the scene unfold gently, not forcing detail, returning to the scene when the mind wanders.

What it isn't: a generic "imagine abundance" prompt. The library has no visualisations of cascading gold coins or glowing bank vaults. Those scenes are too generic for the body to do anything with. The library's visualisations are domestic, daily-life-sized: the reservoir, the wallet, the room.
What makes a scene specific enough
Three things make a visualisation land. The first is one clear central image, "the reservoir," not "flowing water". The body needs an object or a place it can hold.

The second is a point of view. Are you watching the scene from outside, or standing inside it? Both work; the practice is just to pick one and stay there. The visualisation scripts in the library name the point of view at the start.

The third is at least one sensory anchor. The cold of the tap water. The weight of the wallet in the hand. The sound of the rain. One sense, specific enough that you could describe it to someone else. That single anchor makes the scene real to the body.
The sensory anchors used in scripts
The practice scripts use the senses in this order of usefulness, drawn from where most people find imagery easiest to hold:
Sight.
The colour of the water, the size of the room, the look of the object. Start here.
Touch.
The temperature, the weight, the texture against the skin. The second most useful sense for grounding a scene.
Sound.
The rain, the kettle, the room quiet around you. Useful for setting the mood.
Smell and taste.
Strong when they fit (the coffee in the morning scene, the salt air on the holiday scene); skip them when they don't.

If visual imagery is hard for you (aphantasia, or just a busy day), lead with touch or sound instead. Visualisation isn't about vision specifically. It's about holding a sensory scene. The sense that works for your body is the right one.
How to close a session
Let the scene fade. Open the eyes slowly. Notice the room around you for thirty seconds, the chair under you, the light from the window, the breath. Don't jump straight into the next thing.
If a detail stayed with you that you didn't expect, write one line about it. Sometimes the practice gives you a piece of information (a colour, a number, an object) that surprises. Worth keeping.
Where this comes from

Guided visualisation has several roots. Sports psychology has used mental rehearsal since the 1970s. Olympic athletes still picture the race in advance. Hypnotherapy and modern yoga nidra both use guided imagery as a core tool. Cognitive-behavioural therapy uses imagery for anxiety work and for rehearsing new behaviours. The library follows that craft. The way this work pairs visualisation with tapping and energy statements is drawn from Rebecca J Page's MONEY, SLEEP, and MANIFESTING programs.
